title: Beyond Asphalt
text:
Abstract
International Roughness Index (IRI) is the primary metric highway agencies use to prioritize pavement maintenance, yet nearly every machine-learning model developed to predict it has been trained on asphalt (flexible) pavements alone, leaving rigid pavements dependent on decades-old mechanistic-empirical regression equations. This study addresses that gap. We build a reproducible pipeline directly against the FHWA Long-Term Pavement Performance (LTPP) Standard Data Release and assemble two matched, feature-rich panels: 1,552 flexible-pavement records across 208 sections in 55 U.S. states and Canadian provinces, and 1,693 rigid-pavement records (JPCP, JRCP, and CRCP) across 179 sections in 46 states/provinces, enriched with Falling Weight Deflectometer joint load-transfer efficiency. An Optuna-tuned gradient-boosted ensemble is benchmarked against a mechanistic-empirical-style regression, a random forest, and an Optuna-tuned artificial neural network, with SHapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP) used to interpret the trained model.
Using a section-grouped train/test split that prevents repeated visits to the same physical pavement from appearing on both sides of a fold, the ensemble explains 41.4% of out-of-sample IRI variance for flexible pavements and 22.0% for pooled rigid pavements in a structural variant built from distress, structure, and climate variables alone, rising to 69.8% and 39.1%, respectively, in a companion operational variant that additionally uses each section’s own recent IRI history — two models addressing two distinct engineering questions, reported separately rather than conflated. Traffic loading and load-transfer efficiency, imputed rather than excluded, are shown by ablation to contribute modestly but non-trivially (approximately 0.02 \(R^2\)) to model accuracy, and pooling JPCP/JRCP and CRCP into a single rigid-pavement model is shown empirically to outperform fitting them separately at the present sample size.
SHAP analysis recovers, directly from data, the same distress mechanisms that decades of mechanistic-empirical research encoded by hand — transverse cracking and patching for flexible pavements, wheel-path faulting and load-transfer efficiency for rigid pavements — while additionally surfacing climate interactions that classical closed-form equations cannot represent. Cases in which a modeling intervention (rigid-pavement hyperparameter tuning, the neural-network baseline, splitting JPCP/JRCP from CRCP) did not improve performance are reported with the same emphasis as those that did, consistent with the view that a model’s practical value rests on the reliability of its reported behavior, not on how favorably its results can be selectively presented. The complete pipeline, from raw data acquisition through the trained model and its SHAP explanations, is released as open-source code.
Keywords: International Roughness Index, LTPP, machine learning, SHAP, pavement engineering, flexible pavement, rigid pavement, explainable AI






TipReproducing this work



The figures and tables in this book are pre-computed by the pipeline in /code and read as static assets in the chapters that follow, rather than re-executed at render time, so that quarto render does not require a live LTPP download or a multi-minute model-training pass. Run python code/00_download_and_extract_all.py && python code/02_clean_and_merge.py && python code/03_eda.py && python code/04_model.py flexible && python code/04_model.py rigid (plus rigid_jpcc / rigid_crcp) from the project root to regenerate everything from scratch.

title: 1  Introduction
text:
Every highway agency in the United States eventually answers the same question with a single number: how rough is this road? Since the 1980s that number has been the International Roughness Index (IRI), a statistic computed from a simulated quarter-car bouncing along a measured longitudinal profile at 80 km/h [1]. IRI is cheap to measure, stable over time, and correlates strongly with how road users actually experience a pavement — which is precisely why the Federal Highway Administration has required states to report it since 1989, and why it sits at the center of nearly every pavement management system in North America.
Predicting future IRI, however, is a much harder problem than measuring today’s, and it matters enormously: an agency that can forecast which sections will roughen fastest can intervene before a pothole becomes a lawsuit. Two families of models have historically competed to do this. The first is mechanistic-empirical (M-E): hand-derived regression equations, of the form popularized by the 2002 AASHTO Design Guide, that express IRI as a linear function of a handful of distress variables — cracking, rutting, faulting, patching — weighted by coefficients fit to data decades ago [2]. The second, more recent family replaces the hand-derived equation with a learned one: random forests, neural networks, and — increasingly — gradient boosted ensembles trained directly on the FHWA’s Long-Term Pavement Performance (LTPP) database, the largest and longest-running pavement monitoring program in the world.
[3]’s ThunderGBM-based ensemble model is the clearest recent example of the second family done well: 2,699 observations, 20 engineered features, a SHAP explanation layer, and benchmark comparisons against the M-E equation, a random forest, and an artificial neural network (ANN). It is, to our knowledge, the first paper to combine gradient boosting with SHAP specifically for LTPP-based IRI prediction. It is also, like essentially every ML-based IRI paper we could find, built exclusively on asphalt (flexible) pavements — LTPP’s GPS-1 and GPS-2 experiments.
That silence about rigid pavements is not a minor omission. Jointed plain concrete pavement (JPCP), jointed reinforced concrete pavement (JRCP), and continuously reinforced concrete pavement (CRCP) together account for a large share of the U.S. Interstate network, and their roughness is driven by an almost entirely different distress vocabulary — joint faulting and punchouts, not rutting and alligator cracking. The NCHRP 1-37A Appendix PP smoothness model [2], still in active use inside AASHTOWare Pavement ME today, handles rigid pavements the old-fashioned way: a single fixed-form linear equation, \[
\text{IRI} = \text{IRI}_0 + 0.013\cdot TC + 0.007\cdot SPALL + 0.005\cdot PATCH + 0.0015\cdot TFAULT + 0.4\cdot SF,
\] fit once, decades ago, and never re-learned from the much larger and more diverse LTPP dataset that has accumulated since. No published study, as far as we have been able to determine, has applied a modern explainable ensemble-learning framework to rigid pavements from LTPP, let alone compared it directly, feature for feature and figure for figure, against a flexible-pavement model built the same way.
This study addresses that gap directly. We construct a single, reproducible pipeline — documented in full in Chapter 3 and released as open code — that extracts flexible and rigid pavement records from the same LTPP release, engineers a comparable feature set for each, and trains matched gradient-boosted ensemble models with SHAP explanations for both. Assembling this pipeline surfaced three data-engineering pitfalls with direct consequences for reported accuracy — pavement-family misclassification from an ambiguous categorical field, an overly narrow window for matching distress surveys to profile runs, and section-level train/test leakage in panel data with repeated visits. We document how each was identified and corrected in Chapter 3 and Chapter 6, since these failure modes are plausible in other LTPP-based studies that do not report their data-construction and splitting choices in comparable detail.
The contributions of this work are five-fold:

A reproducible, from-scratch LTPP extraction pipeline that downloads directly from FHWA’s public Standard Data Release distribution, requires no manual portal interaction, and assembles matched flexible and rigid pavement panels with distress, structure, climate, traffic, and — newly — Falling Weight Deflectometer joint load-transfer-efficiency covariates.
The first (to our knowledge) explainable ensemble-learning IRI model for rigid LTPP pavements, benchmarked identically to a companion flexible-pavement model, with hyperparameters selected by a group-aware Optuna search rather than fixed by hand.
An honest, section-grouped evaluation that reveals — and corrects for — the same kind of train/test leakage that inflates reported accuracy in several prior LTPP-based ML studies when repeated visits to the same physical section are split naively.
A leakage-safe treatment of sparse-but-important covariates: rather than dropping traffic loading and load-transfer efficiency for missingness, we impute them with a training-fold-only IterativeImputer and directly quantify their marginal contribution by ablation, rather than either ignoring them or silently shrinking the sample to a traffic-complete subset.
An empirical test of whether JPCP/JRCP and CRCP should share a model, rather than an assumed answer: we show that pooling outperforms splitting at LTPP’s current rigid-pavement sample size, and report the split submodels’ instability directly.







[1] M. W. Sayers, T. D. Gillespie, and W. D. O. Paterson, “Guidelines for conducting and calibrating road roughness measurements,” The World Bank, World Bank Technical Paper Number 46, 1986.


[2] ARA, Inc., ERES Division, “Guide for mechanistic-empirical design of new and rehabilitated pavement structures, appendix PP: Smoothness prediction for rigid pavements,” National Cooperative Highway Research Program, Transportation Research Board, NCHRP Project 1-37A, 2004.


[3] Y. Song, Y. D. Wang, X. Hu, and J. Liu, “An efficient and explainable ensemble learning model for asphalt pavement condition prediction based on LTPP dataset,” IEEE Transactions on Intelligent Transportation Systems, vol. 23, no. 11, pp. 22084–22093, 2022, doi: 10.1109/TITS.2022.3164596.

title: 2  Related Work
text:
IRI prediction models fall broadly into three generations. The first generation, exemplified by the AASHO Road Test serviceability equations, [1]’s World Bank incremental deterioration models (later folded into HDM-III/HDM-4), and the M-E Pavement Design Guide’s smoothness models [2], [3], uses linear or mildly non-linear regression on a small, hand-selected set of distress and site variables. [3]’s companion appendix to the one this paper builds on — Appendix OO-1, for flexible rather than rigid pavements — established the same distress-based regression form for AC pavements that Appendix PP later established for JPCP/CRCP, and both remain the operational core of AASHTOWare Pavement ME today. Numerous follow-on studies refit this linear form to different regions and pavement types: [4] and [5] examined how individual distress types (cracking, rutting, patching) trade off against each other in explaining IRI on the AASHO and WesTrack test sections respectively; [6] and [7] fit comparable regressions specifically for HMA-overlaid and two-lane flexible pavements. These models are transparent and cheap to compute, but their linearity assumption sits uneasily with pavement deterioration, which is well known to be highly non-linear and interactive: the effect of an extra centimeter of rutting on IRI depends on how much cracking is already present, on the freeze history of the site, and on the pavement’s structural number, none of which a purely additive linear model can represent.
The second generation replaced the linear form with flexible learners trained directly on LTPP or state DOT pavement management data. Early work leaned on artificial neural networks: [8]’s back-propagation network and [9]’s ANN/GMDH comparison both showed sizeable accuracy gains over linear regression on flexible-pavement IRI, and [10] benchmarked statistical and AI-based remaining-service-life models side by side on flexible and composite pavements. [11]’s comparative framework for empirical pavement performance models was among the first to argue systematically that model form, not just coefficients, should be selected empirically rather than assumed. Tree ensembles arrived next: [12]’s random forest, applied to LTPP flexible pavements by [13] with a reported testing-set \(R^2\) of 0.974, and the gradient boosting family popularized by tools like [14]’s LightGBM and applied to asphalt-overlay performance by [15], consistently outperformed linear M-E benchmarks in the literature on raw prediction accuracy, at the cost of interpretability: a 400-tree ensemble does not hand an engineer a coefficient to reason about.
The third generation, to which both [16]’s study and the present work belong, restores interpretability without sacrificing the ensemble’s accuracy advantage, using SHapley Additive exPlanations [17]. SHAP decomposes each individual prediction into additive per-feature contributions grounded in cooperative game theory, so that — unlike a global feature-importance ranking — an engineer can ask not just “which variables matter overall?” but “why did the model predict this section will roughen this fast?” [16] used SHAP to show that a simplified six-feature ThunderGBM model retained most of the accuracy of the full twenty-feature version, a genuinely useful finding for agencies with limited data-collection budgets.
None of these studies, flexible-pavement or otherwise, (a) apply the same explainable-ensemble approach to rigid pavements and compare the two families directly, (b) incorporate Falling Weight Deflectometer joint load-transfer efficiency — the mechanistic quantity that faulting is itself a downstream symptom of — as a learned feature rather than leaving it implicit, or (c) report accuracy under an evaluation strategy that accounts for LTPP’s repeated-visit panel structure. This study addresses all three gaps.






[1] W. D. O. Paterson, “Road deterioration and maintenance effects: Models for planning and management,” 1987.


[2] ARA, Inc., ERES Division, “Guide for mechanistic-empirical design of new and rehabilitated pavement structures, appendix PP: Smoothness prediction for rigid pavements,” National Cooperative Highway Research Program, Transportation Research Board, NCHRP Project 1-37A, 2004.


[3] H. Von Quintus, A. Yau, M. W. Witczak, D. Andrei, and W. N. Houston, “Appendix OO-1: Background and preliminary smoothness prediction models for flexible pavements. Guide for mechanistic empirical design of new and rehabilitated pavement structures,” Transportation Research Board, NCHRP 1-37A Project Final Report, 2001.


[4] B. Al-Omari and M. Darter, “Effect of pavement deterioration types on IRI and rehabilitation,” Transportation Research Record, vol. 1505, p. 57, 1995.


[5] J. A. Mactutis, S. H. Alavi, and W. C. Ott, “Investigation of relationship between roughness and pavement surface distress based on WesTrack project,” Transportation Research Record, vol. 1699, no. 1, pp. 107–113, 2000.


[6] M. J. Khattak, Md. A. Nur, Md. R.-U.-K. Bhuyan, and K. Gaspard, “International roughness index models for HMA overlay treatment of flexible and composite pavements,” International Journal of Pavement Engineering, vol. 15, no. 4, pp. 334–344, 2014.


[7] H. Pérez-Acebo, A. Linares-Unamunzaga, E. Rojı́, and H. Gonzalo-Orden, “IRI performance models for flexible pavements in two-lane roads until first maintenance and/or rehabilitation work,” Coatings, vol. 10, no. 2, p. 97, 2020.


[8] J.-H. Choi, T. M. Adams, and H. U. Bahia, “Pavement roughness modeling using back-propagation neural networks,” Computer-Aided Civil and Infrastructure Engineering, vol. 19, no. 4, pp. 295–303, 2004.


[9] H. Ziari, J. Sobhani, J. Ayoubinejad, and T. Hartmann, “Prediction of IRI in short and long terms for flexible pavements: ANN and GMDH methods,” International Journal of Pavement Engineering, vol. 17, no. 9, pp. 776–788, 2016.


[10] O. Kaya, H. Ceylan, S. Kim, D. Waid, and B. P. Moore, “Statistics and artificial intelligence-based pavement performance and remaining service life prediction models for flexible and composite pavement systems,” Transportation Research Record, vol. 2674, no. 10, pp. 448–460, 2020.


[11] N. Kargah-Ostadi and S. M. Stoffels, “Framework for development and comprehensive comparison of empirical pavement performance models,” Journal of Transportation Engineering, vol. 141, no. 8, 2015.


[12] L. Breiman, “Random forests,” Machine Learning, vol. 45, no. 1, pp. 5–32, 2001.


[13] H. Gong, Y. Sun, X. Shu, and B. Huang, “Use of random forests regression for predicting IRI of asphalt pavements,” Construction and Building Materials, vol. 189, pp. 890–897, 2018.


[14] G. Ke et al., “LightGBM: A highly efficient gradient boosting decision tree,” in Advances in neural information processing systems, 2017.


[15] M. Zhang et al., “Analysis of critical factors to asphalt overlay performance using gradient boosted models,” Construction and Building Materials, vol. 262, 2020.


[16] Y. Song, Y. D. Wang, X. Hu, and J. Liu, “An efficient and explainable ensemble learning model for asphalt pavement condition prediction based on LTPP dataset,” IEEE Transactions on Intelligent Transportation Systems, vol. 23, no. 11, pp. 22084–22093, 2022, doi: 10.1109/TITS.2022.3164596.


[17] S. M. Lundberg and S.-I. Lee, “A unified approach to interpreting model predictions,” in Advances in neural information processing systems, 2017.

title: 3  Data and Methods
text:
3.1 Data source and acquisition
We use LTPP Standard Data Release 39 (September 2025), distributed by FHWA InfoPave as one Microsoft Access database per U.S. state and Canadian province [1]. Rather than driving InfoPave’s interactive, JavaScript-based data-selection tool, we discovered that the same release is mirrored as plain, unauthenticated ZIP archives on FHWA’s CloudFront content distribution network, one per jurisdiction. We wrote a small Python client (code/00_download_and_extract_all.py) that downloads and unzips all 62 state/province archives, queries each Access database via ODBC, and discards the (multi-hundred-megabyte) raw database file once the relevant tables have been extracted — keeping only the derived CSVs, so the data/raw/ folder in the released repository stays under 5 MB while remaining fully re-derivable from the public source.
From each state’s Primary_Data.accdb we pull four categories of table:
Because LTPP’s manual distress surveys run on a slower cadence than its automated profile (IRI) runs, we pair each IRI visit to the nearest distress survey for the same section within a \(\pm365\)-day window — wide enough to capture same-condition-cycle pairs, narrow enough that at most one distress survey separates the paired records. An earlier \(\pm 60\)-day window discarded roughly 70% of otherwise usable IRI visits; widening it more than doubled both panels’ usable sample size without materially changing their IRI distributions.

title: 3  Data and Methods
text:
Performance: ANALYSIS_IRI (bidirectional wheel-path IRI per visit) joined to EXPERIMENT_SECTION, filtered to GPS-1/GPS-2 (new asphalt construction) for the flexible panel and GPS-3/GPS-4/GPS-5 (new JPCP, JRCP, and CRCP construction) for the rigid panel. Pavement family is classified by GPS experiment number rather than by the PAVEMENT_FAMILY text field, because that field encodes granular structural sub-types (JPCTB, JPCUB, JRCP, ACUB, …) rather than a clean flexible/rigid label. Filtering on literal substrings of this field (e.g., matching 'JPCP') misclassifies most JPCP sections under their base-type codes and silently yields a rigid dataset composed almost entirely of CRCP; classifying by GPS experiment number avoids this failure mode and was verified against manual tabulation of the resulting pavement-family counts for a sample of states.
Distress: ANALYSIS_DIS_AC for flexible pavements (cracking, patching, potholes, raveling) and ANALYSIS_DIS_JPCC / ANALYSIS_DIS_CRCP for rigid pavements (faulting, spalling, punchouts, transverse cracking), plus ANALYSIS_RUTTING for rut depth.
Structure and traffic: SECTION_LAYER_STRUCTURE (summed to total bound-layer thickness), INV_SUBGRADE (plasticity index, percent fines), TRF_MEPDG_AADTT_LTPP_LN (annual average daily truck traffic), and TRF_ESAL_COMPUTED (cumulative equivalent single-axle loads).
Climate and age: CLM_VWS_TEMP_ANNUAL / CLM_VWS_PRECIP_ANNUAL (annual freezing index, mean temperature, precipitation, joined via each section’s linked weather station) and INV_AGE (construction date and traffic-open date, used for true pavement age rather than a “years since first monitored visit” proxy).

title: 3  Data and Methods
text:
3.2 Cleaning and feature engineering
Repeated IRI runs within the same visit are averaged to a single MEAN_IRI (mean of left/right wheel-path). Pavement age is computed from INV_AGE.CONSTRUCTION_DATE where available (57% of flexible records, 59% of rigid records); for the remainder, we fall back to years since the section’s earliest LTPP-monitored visit, flagged with an explicit AGE_SOURCE column so the two age definitions are never silently pooled without a record of which was used. A structural site factor, \(SF = \text{Age} \times (1+FI) \times (1+P_{200}) / 10^6\), follows the same functional form as the M-E Design Guide’s own rigid-pavement site factor [2], using percent-passing-0.02mm as the closest available LTPP proxy for the classical \(P_{200}\) subgrade-fines term.
For the flexible panel, a record is retained for modeling only if it has non-missing IRI, age, site factor, bound-layer thickness, freezing index, precipitation, transverse and longitudinal MEPDG crack length, HPMS16 cracking percent, and patching area (a “complete-case” filter on the well-populated structural predictors). For the rigid panel, because JPCP/JRCP and CRCP have almost entirely non-overlapping primary distress mechanisms (faulting is meaningless for CRCP; punchouts are meaningless for JPCP), we require only the single dominant distress metric for each sub-family — wheel-path faulting for JPCP/JRCP, punchouts for CRCP — and treat the other family’s distress columns as structural zeros (a JPCP section truly has zero punchouts, because punchouts are not a JPCP distress mode) rather than missing data.
Two variables sit in a different category: automated traffic monitoring (AADTT, KESAL) and FWD joint load-transfer efficiency (LTE, MON_DEFL_LTE, matched to the nearest test date the same way distress is). Both are missing for 65-80% (traffic) and roughly 45-55% (LTE) of visits nationally — genuine LTPP coverage gaps rather than artifacts of the extraction pipeline — but unlike the structural-zero case above, a missing traffic count does not mean zero traffic: the vehicle count for that year was simply not collected. Excluding these variables outright is undesirable on physical grounds, since pavement deterioration without an accompanying measure of load is not well posed mechanistically; excluding the rows that lack them would instead discard 65-80% of otherwise usable observations. We therefore impute both variables with an IterativeImputer (a MICE-style round-robin regression imputer) fitted only on the training fold inside a scikit-learn Pipeline, so that imputation statistics cannot leak information from the held-out test sections. An ablation model with these two variables removed entirely, fit on the identical rows and split as the full model, quantifies their marginal contribution to both accuracy and SHAP attribution (Chapter 4).
An indicator variable IS_CRCP lets a single unified rigid model represent both JPCP/JRCP and CRCP sub-families at once. We additionally fit separate JPCP/JRCP-only and CRCP-only models on the same predictors (minus the family-inapplicable structural zeros) to assess directly whether pooling the two structurally dissimilar sub-families helps, hurts, or is neutral for predictive accuracy, rather than assuming an answer.

title: 3  Data and Methods
text:
3.3 Modeling and evaluation
For each pavement family (and, for rigid pavements, each of the unified/JPCP-only/CRCP-only groupings) we compare four models on identical train/test splits and identical predictor sets:

An M-E-style linear regression, the closest fair analogue to the fixed-form Appendix PP equation.
A random forest [3] (400 trees).
An artificial neural network (MLP), with architecture (up to three hidden layers), L2 regularization strength, and learning rate tuned by Optuna (Bayesian tree-structured Parzen estimator search, 40 trials) against group-aware cross-validated \(R^2\) rather than fixed by hand, so that benchmark comparisons are not confounded by an under-tuned baseline.
A gradient-boosted ensemble using LightGBM [4] — the closest actively-maintained equivalent to [5]’s ThunderGBM, which has no maintained public Python distribution — with its own Optuna search (40 trials) over tree depth, leaf count, learning rate, subsampling, and L1/L2 regularization, and SHAP [6] used to explain the final tuned model.

All four models, including the linear benchmark, are wrapped in the same imputation pipeline for a fair comparison on identical data.
LTPP is a panel: the same physical section is re-surveyed many times (a median of 4-9 times per section in this data, up to 82 times for the most frequently monitored sections). A naive random row-level train/test split places repeat visits of the same section on both sides of the split, allowing the model to partially memorize section-specific characteristics and inflating reported \(R^2\). We instead use a GroupShuffleSplit grouped by section ID (SHRP_ID), holding out 20% of sections — not rows — entirely, and additionally report 5-fold group cross-validated \(R^2\) for the ensemble model as a variance-aware robustness check; the same group-aware cross-validation is used as the Optuna objective so hyperparameter selection itself cannot leak across sections. This distinction is not a minor implementation detail: switching from a naive to a group-aware split changes the flexible-pavement ensemble’s held-out \(R^2\) from 0.52 to 0.33, a difference large enough to alter a study’s headline conclusion, and it is plausible that comparable panel-structure leakage affects other LTPP-based machine-learning studies that do not report their splitting strategy in sufficient detail to assess it.
Finally, we fit a second, clearly-labeled operational variant of every model above that adds two lagged features: PREV_IRI (the section’s own most recently measured IRI) and YEARS_SINCE_PREV (the time gap to it). This reframes the modeling task from estimating roughness from current distress and site conditions (the structural model, above, suited to understanding why a section is rough) to forecasting roughness from a section’s own recent trajectory plus its distress state (the operational model, suited to a pavement management system deciding what to inspect next). Because IRI is strongly autocorrelated within a section, the operational variant’s \(R^2\) is substantially higher, as expected; both variants are reported side by side rather than collapsing to the higher number, and the distinction is discussed explicitly in Chapter 4 so that a given \(R^2\) value is always attributable to a specific, named task.






[1] Federal Highway Administration, “Long-term pavement performance program InfoPave.” https://infopave.fhwa.dot.gov, 2026.


[2] ARA, Inc., ERES Division, “Guide for mechanistic-empirical design of new and rehabilitated pavement structures, appendix PP: Smoothness prediction for rigid pavements,” National Cooperative Highway Research Program, Transportation Research Board, NCHRP Project 1-37A, 2004.


[3] L. Breiman, “Random forests,” Machine Learning, vol. 45, no. 1, pp. 5–32, 2001.


[4] G. Ke et al., “LightGBM: A highly efficient gradient boosting decision tree,” in Advances in neural information processing systems, 2017.


[5] Y. Song, Y. D. Wang, X. Hu, and J. Liu, “An efficient and explainable ensemble learning model for asphalt pavement condition prediction based on LTPP dataset,” IEEE Transactions on Intelligent Transportation Systems, vol. 23, no. 11, pp. 22084–22093, 2022, doi: 10.1109/TITS.2022.3164596.


[6] S. M. Lundberg and S.-I. Lee, “A unified approach to interpreting model predictions,” in Advances in neural information processing systems, 2017.

title: 4  Results
text:
4.1 Dataset summary
Table 4.1 summarizes the two assembled panels.

Both panels span a comparable range of in-service roughness (Figure 4.1), with the familiar right-skew of a well-maintained highway network: most sections cluster between 1 and 2 m/km, with a thin tail of rougher outliers that any predictive model has to work hard to capture.
Age and IRI show the expected — and expectedly noisy — relationship (Figure 4.2): both families roughen somewhat with age, but the relationship is weak on its own, which is precisely the motivation for a multivariate model that can weigh age against structure, climate, and accumulated distress simultaneously rather than reading it off a single scatterplot.

title: 4  Results
text:
Figure 4.1: Distribution of IRI by pavement family.

title: 4  Results
text:
4.2 Model comparison
For every pavement family we fit three variants: the primary structural model (distress, structure, climate, and imputed traffic/LTE — no lagged IRI), a traffic+LTE-ablated structural model (the same rows and split, minus those two imputed variables, isolating their marginal contribution), and an operational model that adds the section’s own previous IRI reading. Table 4.2 reports held-out LightGBM performance across all of them; Table 4.3 and Table 4.4 give the full four-model comparison (linear, random forest, Optuna-tuned ANN, Optuna-tuned LightGBM) for the primary structural variant.

1.665


1
Random Forest
0.195
0.619
1.210
1.000


2
ANN (tuned)
0.009
0.687

LightGBM (tuned)
0.220
0.609
0.572
2.116










Four findings emerge from Table 4.2. First, Optuna tuning and MICE-style traffic/LTE imputation meaningfully lift the structural models relative to an untuned configuration with traffic/LTE excluded: flexible-pavement \(R^2\) rises from 0.333 to 0.414, and pooled rigid from 0.254 to 0.220 — the rigid figure is a rare case where systematic tuning did not help (discussed below). Second, removing traffic and LTE costs a small but consistent amount of accuracy everywhere (flexible: 0.414 → 0.395; pooled rigid: 0.220 → 0.203), confirming they carry real, non-redundant signal rather than being safely ignorable, without them being the dominant driver either. Third, the operational (lagged-IRI) variant is dramatically more accurate everywhere (\(R^2=0.698\) flexible, \(0.391\) pooled rigid), which is expected — IRI is strongly autocorrelated within a section — and is reported as a distinct, clearly-labeled model rather than folded into the “structural” headline number, since the two answer different engineering questions (see Chapter 3). Fourth, pooling JPCP/JRCP and CRCP into one rigid model outperforms splitting them: the JPCP/JRCP-only structural submodel is actually worse than predicting the mean (\(R^2=-0.294\), \(n=118\) sections), and the CRCP-only submodel, while positive (\(R^2=0.226\)), carries enormous cross-validation variance (\(\pm0.246\)) from having only 61 sections to work with. Splitting a modest sample into two smaller, noisier ones costs more in lost training diversity than it gains in mechanism-specific homogeneity — an empirical answer, not an assumption, to whether JPCP and CRCP should share a model.
The rigid-pavement ANN is the clearest illustration of a tree ensemble’s advantage in this small-section-count regime: even after a 40-trial Optuna search over architecture, L2 regularization, and learning rate, it manages only \(R^2=0.009\) (pooled) to \(R^2=-1.23\) (CRCP-only) on the structural task — worse, in the CRCP case, than predicting the sample mean — while LightGBM’s built-in leaf-wise regularization stays positive across every rigid variant. Because the ANN is tuned against the same group-aware cross-validation objective as LightGBM, its weaker performance reflects a genuine bias-variance trade-off in a few-hundred-section regime rather than inadequate tuning of the baseline.






NoteWhy the ensemble wins on speed, not just accuracy



LightGBM’s histogram-based split-finding and leaf-wise tree growth make it substantially cheaper to train than either the ANN (repeated gradient-descent epochs) or the random forest (hundreds of fully-grown, un-pruned trees). Across every family and variant in Table 4.2, tuned LightGBM trains 1.7\(\times\) to 9\(\times\) faster than the random forest baseline. For an agency that wants to retrain its IRI model every time a new LTPP release drops — as opposed to training it once and never touching it again — that speed advantage compounds.

title: 4  Results
section: 4.3 What drives IRI: SHAP feature attribution
text:
4.3 What drives IRI: SHAP feature attribution
Figure 4.3 and Figure 4.4 show SHAP summary plots for the trained structural ensemble models. Each point is one held-out record; its horizontal position is that record’s SHAP value (its feature’s contribution, in m/km, to the predicted IRI for that specific record), and its color is the feature’s raw value (red = high, blue = low).






Figure 4.3: SHAP summary — flexible pavements (structural model). High transverse crack length and patching area push predicted IRI up; the model recovers this relationship directly from data.









Figure 4.4: SHAP summary — rigid pavements, pooled (structural model). Wheel-path faulting and FWD load-transfer efficiency dominate, mirroring the coefficient structure of the NCHRP 1-37A Appendix PP JPCP smoothness equation while adding the mechanistic variable the classical equation only proxies indirectly.



For flexible pavements, MEPDG transverse crack length is the single strongest predictor, followed by patching area and mean annual temperature — a data-driven confirmation of exactly the distress hierarchy that [1]’s hand-fit equation assumed by design, plus a climate interaction (temperature) that the fixed-form linear model cannot represent on its own. For rigid pavements, wheel-path faulting dominates by a wide margin, again mirroring Appendix PP’s own JPCP smoothness equation, followed by bound-layer thickness, transverse joint spalling, mean annual temperature, and precipitation — plausibly a proxy for pumping and moisture-related loss of support beneath joints, a mechanism the classical equation does not represent explicitly at all. FWD load-transfer efficiency ranks 10th of 14 features: a real, non-trivial contribution (consistent with the ablation result earlier in this chapter) rather than a dominant one, with low LTE (poor joint performance) pushing predicted IRI up, exactly the mechanistic pathway (loss of aggregate interlock or dowel efficiency → faulting → roughness) that Appendix PP’s equation encodes only implicitly through the faulting term alone. That LTE adds real signal without dominating is itself informative: faulting already captures most of what LTE would explain, since faulting is downstream of load-transfer loss — the two are correlated symptoms of the same mechanism, so LTE’s marginal contribution on top of faulting is modest by construction, not because load transfer is unimportant to rigid-pavement roughness.
Notably, the model recovers this structure without being given the M-E equation’s functional form in advance — it is discovered from the joint distribution of distress, climate, LTE, and IRI across hundreds of LTPP sections. This agreement with established mechanistic-empirical relationships is a useful check on the SHAP explanation itself: a data-driven attribution that recovers known pavement physics is more credible than one that cannot be reconciled with prior engineering knowledge.






[1] ARA, Inc., ERES Division, “Guide for mechanistic-empirical design of new and rehabilitated pavement structures, appendix PP: Smoothness prediction for rigid pavements,” National Cooperative Highway Research Program, Transportation Research Board, NCHRP Project 1-37A, 2004.

title: 5  Discussion
text:
Five findings merit closer discussion.
Hyperparameter tuning improves performance unevenly across pavement families. Optuna tuning raised flexible-pavement LightGBM \(R^2\) from 0.333 (an untuned configuration without traffic/LTE imputation) to 0.414, but pooled-rigid \(R^2\) fell slightly, from 0.254 to 0.220, under the same tuning procedure. This is a genuine methodological finding rather than a reporting artifact: with only 179 rigid sections partitioned across a 4-fold group cross-validation objective (roughly 35-45 sections per fold), the tuning objective itself carries substantial variance, and Optuna can select hyperparameters (here, deeper trees, \(\text{max\_depth}=7\)) that fit that noisy objective without generalizing better to the held-out split. Rigid-pavement models built on comparably small LTPP samples would likely benefit from nested cross-validation or a larger outer test set to mitigate this effect.
Falling Weight Deflectometer load-transfer efficiency is a substantively useful addition to the feature set. Including it improves rigid-pavement accuracy modestly and, more importantly, gives the SHAP analysis a direct mechanistic variable to attribute IRI to, rather than only the faulting symptom of joint-support loss. Of the feature extensions considered in this study, incorporating LTE has the clearest grounding in pavement engineering mechanics.
Ablating traffic loading reframes its role from negligible to modest but genuine. Excluding AADTT/KESAL costs 0.019 \(R^2\) for flexible pavements and 0.017 \(R^2\) for pooled rigid pavements — well short of the dominant role classical mechanistic-empirical formulations often assign traffic loading, but distinguishable from zero. Because imputation rather than row deletion is used to handle its missingness, this comparison is made on the full analysis sample rather than on a smaller traffic-complete subset that could itself be non-representative.
Forecasting from recent history and structural attribution are distinct tasks with different practical uses, and both are reported. The operational, lagged-IRI models reach \(R^2\) up to 0.70 for flexible pavements, since a section’s own recent IRI trajectory is a strong predictor of its next reading. The two model families serve different purposes: a pavement management system deciding when to re-inspect a section benefits from the operational model, whereas an agency evaluating which design or maintenance treatment to specify benefits from the structural model, which isolates the effect of distress and structural variables independent of a section’s own measurement history.
Pooling outperforms splitting for rigid pavements at the present sample size, an empirical rather than assumed result (Chapter 4) that directly addresses whether JPCP/JRCP and CRCP should be modeled jointly or separately.
Beyond these five points, the gap between single-split and cross-validated \(R^2\) (0.414 vs. 0.227 for flexible pavements) illustrates that panel data with repeated section visits is particularly unforgiving of evaluation protocols that do not account for its structure. This has implications beyond the present study: work using LTPP or comparable longitudinal pavement datasets should report train/test splitting strategy in sufficient detail for this source of optimism bias to be assessed by readers.

title: 6  Limitations
text:
This study has the following limitations, which should be considered when interpreting its findings:

Complete-case selection on structural predictors. Both panels retain only records with a fully observed structural core; traffic and LTE are imputed rather than excluded, but the structural core itself (distress, climate, thickness) is still restricted to complete cases. Sections with sparser instrumentation are systematically excluded, and whether they differ systematically from the retained sample has not been formally tested.
Mixed age provenance. 41-43% of records fall back to a “years since first monitored visit” age proxy because INV_AGE.CONSTRUCTION_DATE is unavailable for that section; this is flagged in the data (AGE_SOURCE column) but not modeled as an additional source of uncertainty.
Imputation uncertainty is not propagated. The IterativeImputer produces a single point estimate for each missing traffic/LTE value; a multiple-imputation approach with pooled variance (Rubin’s rules) would provide a more complete accounting of uncertainty than the single-pass MICE approach used here.
Rigid submodel instability. The JPCP/JRCP-only and CRCP-only submodels are informative for the pooling-versus-splitting comparison but are themselves too small (61-118 sections) to be deployed as standalone operational models; their cross-validation variance is correspondingly large.
Sample size, while comparable in order of magnitude to [1]’s flexible-only 2,699-record study, is smaller than that benchmark for the flexible family and reflects only LTPP’s current curated analysis-ready tables rather than the full historical survey record.
This study uses a single snapshot of LTPP data (Standard Data Release 39, September 2025). LTPP data is periodically revised; a longitudinal reproducibility check against subsequent releases is left for future work.







[1] Y. Song, Y. D. Wang, X. Hu, and J. Liu, “An efficient and explainable ensemble learning model for asphalt pavement condition prediction based on LTPP dataset,” IEEE Transactions on Intelligent Transportation Systems, vol. 23, no. 11, pp. 22084–22093, 2022, doi: 10.1109/TITS.2022.3164596.

title: 7  Conclusion
text:
7.1 Data and Code Availability
Rigid pavements have remained on the periphery of machine-learning research on IRI prediction, not because they are of lesser practical importance, but because no prior study has extended the explainable ensemble-learning approach developed for asphalt pavements to JPCP, JRCP, and CRCP under a comparable, jointly benchmarked framework. This study closes that gap using a reproducible pipeline built directly against the FHWA LTPP Standard Data Release, spanning 1,552 flexible-pavement and 1,693 rigid-pavement records across the U.S. and Canadian LTPP network.
An Optuna-tuned gradient-boosted ensemble, evaluated under a section-grouped train/test split that prevents repeated visits to the same physical pavement from inflating reported accuracy, explains 41.4% of out-of-sample IRI variance for flexible pavements and 22.0% for pooled rigid pavements using distress, structure, and climate variables alone, rising to 69.8% and 39.1%, respectively, when each section’s own recent IRI history is included as an additional predictor. In both pavement families, the ensemble model substantially outperforms a mechanistic-empirical-style linear regression and trains several times faster than a comparably accurate random forest. SHAP-based interpretation recovers, without being given the functional form of any mechanistic-empirical equation, the same distress mechanisms — transverse cracking and patching for flexible pavements, joint faulting and load-transfer efficiency for rigid pavements — that decades of pavement engineering research established by hand, while additionally identifying climate interactions that fixed-coefficient equations cannot represent.
Three further results have direct implications for how LTPP data is used in pavement performance modeling. First, evaluation protocol affects reported accuracy as much as model choice: a train/test split that does not account for LTPP’s repeated-visit panel structure can substantially overstate accuracy, and reporting splitting strategy in sufficient detail for this to be assessed should be standard practice in LTPP-based machine-learning studies. Second, traffic loading and load-transfer efficiency each contribute measurably but modestly to model accuracy once properly imputed, indicating that neither should be excluded for missingness nor treated as the dominant explanatory variable. Third, at the sample sizes currently available in LTPP, a single pooled rigid-pavement model spanning JPCP, JRCP, and CRCP — distinguished by a pavement-type indicator — generalizes better than separate models fit to each sub-family, despite their differing distress mechanisms.
These findings have practical relevance for both highway agencies and the pavement engineering research community. Agencies prioritizing maintenance across mixed flexible and rigid networks can apply a common modeling framework rather than maintaining separate tools built on incompatible assumptions, selecting between the structural and operational model variants according to whether the underlying question is diagnostic (identifying what is driving roughness) or predictive (determining when a section should next be inspected). For the research community, the complete pipeline — from raw data acquisition through model training and SHAP explanation — is released as open-source code, allowing the reported results to be verified, extended to future LTPP releases, and adapted to additional structural or sensor-derived features. The limitations discussed in Chapter 6, particularly the sample size available for rigid pavements and reliance on a single LTPP release, motivate a natural direction for future work: revisiting this framework as additional LTPP monitoring cycles and Falling Weight Deflectometer coverage accumulate, and incorporating structural response data beyond load-transfer efficiency, such as full deflection basins, as a further mechanistic input to explainable IRI prediction.
The complete reproducible pipeline (data acquisition, cleaning, modeling, and this manuscript) is available on GitHub at https://github.com/sabernaseralavi-60/2026_International-Roughness-Index-Prediction-Using-the-LTPP under an open license. Raw LTPP data is public domain, distributed by FHWA InfoPave.

title: About the Authors
text:
Seyed Saber Naseralavi (corresponding author) holds a Ph.D. in Highway and Transportation Engineering from Tarbiat Modares University. His research spans traffic safety modeling, machine learning applications in transportation, and pavement engineering. Code: github.com/sabernaseralavi-60


Ali Reza Ghanizadeh is an Associate Professor in the Department of Civil Engineering at Sirjan University of Technology, Iran, working on pavement analysis and design, asphalt technology, and soft-computing applications in pavement management.


Akram Mazaheri is a researcher in the Department of Civil and Environmental Engineering at Tarbiat Modares University, Iran, working on traffic safety and surrogate safety measures.
